text: Current non-targeted liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) approaches in metabolomics rely heavily on the use of high-sensitivity detectors and employ a suite of experimental approaches to elucidate the composition of small molecules present in environmental samples. Of these, the routine application of data-dependent acquisition (DDA) of product ion spectra is almost ubiquitous. A proper selection of parameters in DDA is necessary to achieve the optimal performance in terms of mass spectrum resolution and maximum description of the analytes present in the sample. Here, we investigated how different parameters affect the performance of Q Exactive DDA (10 parameters tested) when analysing representative natural organic matter derived from different marine, fresh-water and soil environments. We used classical data analysis workflows in conjunction with data processed with MZmine using a feature-based approach for molecular networking via GNPS and then evaluated network properties in terms of number of nodes, clustering patterns and spectrum features (annotation/singleton rates, %MS/MS placed and %spectrum coverage). Overall, parameters like automatic gain control, microscans, mass resolving power and dynamic exclusion showed the most significant impact, while others, such as collision energy, TopN and isolation width exhibited moderate effects on the characteristics of the mass spectra. By contrast, other parameters such as the apex trigger, monoisotopic selection and isotopic exclusion showed minor effects on mass spectra. Basic optimization suggestions of the parameters based on the findings are given for beginners in the use of the Q Exactive, together with a first guess for possible parameter optimisation on other types of samples or MS equipment.
